question_text: Realisation Account is a:
explanation: Realisation Account is a nominal account prepared at the time of dissolution. It is used to find the net profit or loss on realisation of assets and payment of liabilities. It collects all asset and liability transfers on the debit and credit sides respectively, along with realisation expenses and amounts received/paid.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Real accounts relate to assets. Realisation A/c is not an asset — it's used to compute profit/loss on dissolution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the account with the assets transferred to it.

elimination_hints.hint: Personal accounts relate to persons/entities. Realisation A/c doesn't represent a person.
Valuation account is not a standard classification. Realisation A/c is nominal as it determines profit or loss.

question_text: Which of the following is transferred to the debit side of Realisation Account?
explanation: All assets (at book value) are transferred to the debit side of Realisation Account. Entry: Realisation A/c Dr., To Various Asset A/cs. Cash/Bank balance is NOT transferred to Realisation — it remains in the Cash/Bank Account to facilitate payments.
subtopic: Items on debit side of Realisation A/c
subject: accountancy

options.text: All assets (except cash/bank) at book value

elimination_hints.hint: Liabilities go to the CREDIT side of Realisation A/c (they are being settled/transferred out).
elimination_hints.misconception: Putting liabilities on the wrong side.

elimination_hints.hint: Cash/Bank is NOT transferred to Realisation. It stays separate as it's the medium for settling everything.
Partners' capital balances are settled separately. They don't go through Realisation Account.

question_text: Which of the following is transferred to the credit side of Realisation Account?
explanation: All external liabilities (creditors, bills payable, loans, etc.) are transferred to the credit side of Realisation Account at their book value. Entry: Various Liability A/cs Dr., To Realisation A/c. Also, the amount received from sale of assets is credited to Realisation.
subtopic: Items on credit side of Realisation A/c
subject: accountancy

options.text: All external liabilities at book value

elimination_hints.hint: Assets at book value go to the DEBIT side (transferred out of individual asset accounts into Realisation).
Realisation expenses are debited to Realisation A/c (they're a cost), not credited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Putting expenses on the credit side.

elimination_hints.hint: Partners' loan accounts are settled separately — they don't go through Realisation Account.

question_text: On dissolution, assets with a book value of ₹5,00,000 were sold for ₹4,20,000. The Realisation Account shows:
explanation: Assets at book value = ₹5,00,000 (debit side). Sale proceeds = ₹4,20,000 (credit side). Net = ₹5,00,000 − ₹4,20,000 = ₹80,000 loss on realisation. The debit side exceeds the credit side, indicating a loss.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Sold for LESS than book value means a loss, not a profit. ₹4,20,000 < ₹5,00,000.
₹4,20,000 is the sale proceeds, not the loss. Loss = Book value − Sale proceeds = ₹80,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using sale proceeds as the loss figure.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,00,000 is the book value, not a profit. Profit/loss is the difference.

question_text: Realisation expenses paid by the firm are:
explanation: Realisation expenses (auctioneer's fees, legal charges, etc.) paid by the firm are debited to Realisation Account and credited to Cash/Bank Account. They are costs of the dissolution process. Entry: Realisation A/c Dr., To Cash/Bank A/c.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debited to Partners' Capital Accounts in PSR

elimination_hints.hint: Expenses are costs (losses). Costs are debited, not credited, to Realisation.
Expenses go through Realisation A/c first. The net loss (including expenses) is then distributed to partners.
On dissolution, P&L Account is not used for dissolution expenses. Everything goes through Realisation A/c.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using normal P&L treatment during dissolution.

question_text: When a partner agrees to bear the realisation expenses and is paid a fixed amount for it, the entry is:
explanation: When a partner agrees to bear expenses and receives a fixed sum: the firm debits Realisation A/c and credits the partner's Capital A/c with the agreed amount. The partner then pays the actual expenses from their own pocket. The firm's liability is limited to the agreed sum regardless of actual expenses.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Realisation A/c Dr. → To Cash A/c
Partner's Capital A/c Dr. → To Realisation A/c
Realisation A/c Dr. → To Partner's Capital A/c
Cash A/c Dr. → To Partner's Capital A/c

elimination_hints.hint: This entry is for when the FIRM pays expenses directly. Here the partner is handling it for a fixed fee.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing direct firm payment with partner-borne expenses.

elimination_hints.hint: This debits the partner — but the partner is EARNING the fee, so they should be credited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing debit and credit for the partner.

elimination_hints.hint: Cash is involved only when the partner is paid in cash. The standard entry credits the capital account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using cash instead of capital account.

question_text: On dissolution, creditors with a book value of ₹80,000 were paid ₹75,000 in full settlement. The Realisation Account records:
explanation: Creditors at book value ₹80,000 → credited to Realisation (transfer). Payment of ₹75,000 → debited to Realisation, credited to Cash. Net effect on Realisation: Credit ₹80,000 − Debit ₹75,000 = ₹5,000 profit (saved ₹5,000 on settlement).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Paying LESS than owed is a gain for the firm, not a loss. The firm saved ₹5,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a saving as a loss.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹75,000 is the amount paid, not the loss. The loss/gain is the difference between book value and payment.
₹80,000 is the book value of creditors. The gain is only the difference (₹5,000).

question_text: A, B, and C share profits in 2:2:1. On dissolution: Assets (book value ₹8,00,000) sold for ₹6,50,000. Creditors (book value ₹3,00,000) paid ₹2,80,000. Realisation expenses ₹20,000. The profit/loss on realisation is:
explanation:
Realisation A/c:
Debit side: Assets ₹8,00,000 + Expenses ₹20,000 + Creditors paid ₹2,80,000 = ₹11,00,000.
Credit side: Creditors ₹3,00,000 + Sale proceeds ₹6,50,000 = ₹9,50,000.
Loss = ₹11,00,000 − ₹9,50,000 = ₹1,50,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,70,000 may result from adding expenses twice or not accounting for creditors' savings.
₹1,30,000 = 8,00,000 − 6,50,000 − 20,000. This ignores the creditors' saving of ₹20,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Only considering asset loss and expenses.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,00,000 doesn't account for the creditors' settlement gain. Loss on assets = ₹1,50,000, expenses ₹20,000, but creditors saved ₹20,000.

question_text: Profit or loss on realisation is transferred to partners' capital accounts in their:
explanation: The profit or loss determined by the Realisation Account is transferred to all partners' capital accounts in their profit-sharing ratio. If profit: Partners' Capital A/c credited. If loss: Partners' Capital A/c debited.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Capital ratio is used for Garner v Murray insolvency loss, not for normal realisation profit/loss.
Equal ratio applies only if the partnership deed specifies equal sharing. Default is PSR.
Loan balances have no role in distributing realisation profit/loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using loan ratio for profit distribution.

question_text: On dissolution, when a partner takes over an asset at an agreed value, the entry is:
explanation: When a partner takes over an asset at an agreed value, the asset is effectively 'sold' to the partner. Entry: Partner's Capital A/c Dr. (agreed value), To Realisation A/c (agreed value). The asset is removed from Realisation at the agreed value (not book value), and the partner's capital is reduced.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Realisation A/c Dr. → To Partner's Capital A/c
Asset A/c Dr. → To Partner's Capital A/c
Partner's Capital A/c Dr. → To Cash A/c
Partner's Capital A/c Dr. → To Realisation A/c

elimination_hints.hint: This entry credits the partner — meaning they receive money. But the partner is taking an asset, so their capital should be reduced (debited).
Individual Asset A/c has already been closed by transfer to Realisation. No separate Asset A/c entry is needed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that assets are already in Realisation.

elimination_hints.hint: The partner doesn't pay cash to the firm. The asset's value is adjusted against their capital account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating partner takeover as a cash transaction.

question_text: Cash and bank balances are transferred to the Realisation Account on dissolution.
explanation: FALSE. Cash and bank balances are NOT transferred to Realisation Account. They remain in the Cash/Bank Account because cash is the medium through which all payments (to creditors, for expenses, to partners) are made during dissolution.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Cash is the settlement medium. If transferred to Realisation, there would be no way to make payments. It stays separate.

question_text: Partners' loan accounts are closed through the Realisation Account.
explanation: FALSE. Partners' loan accounts are settled separately — they are paid out of cash. Entry: Partner's Loan A/c Dr., To Cash/Bank A/c. They do NOT pass through Realisation Account. Only external liabilities go through Realisation.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Partners' loans are settled directly through cash. They are not 'realised' — they are simply repaid.

question_text: On dissolution, all assets (except _______ and _______) are transferred to the debit side of Realisation Account.
explanation: All assets except Cash/Bank and Fictitious Assets (like debit balance of P&L Account) are transferred to Realisation Account. Cash stays as the payment medium. Fictitious assets (accumulated losses) are written off directly to partners' capital accounts in PSR.
subject: accountancy

question_text: When an asset is taken over by a partner on dissolution, the partner's _______ Account is debited and _______ Account is credited.
explanation: When a partner takes an asset: Partner's Capital A/c Dr. (reducing their capital claim since they're taking value in kind), To Realisation A/c Cr. (treating it as if the asset was 'sold' to the partner at agreed value).
subtopic: Entry for asset taken by partner
subject: accountancy

question_text:
Assertion (A): Cash/Bank balance is not transferred to the Realisation Account on dissolution.
Reason (R): Cash is the medium through which all dissolution payments are made.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Cash/Bank stays separate because it is needed to make payments (to creditors, for expenses, to partners). If it were transferred to Realisation, there would be no cash available for settlements.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R is precisely the reason for A — cash's role as the payment medium is exactly why it's kept separate.
R is factually true — all payments during dissolution (creditors, expenses, partners) are made through cash.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting that cash is the payment medium.

elimination_hints.hint: A is a standard rule — cash is always excluded from Realisation Account.

question_text: On dissolution of A, B, C firm (PSR 3:2:1): Assets ₹10,00,000 (book value), Creditors ₹4,00,000. Assets realised ₹8,50,000. Creditors paid ₹3,90,000. A bore realisation expenses (actual ₹15,000) for an agreed fee of ₹20,000. The realisation loss distributed to B is:
explanation:
Realisation A/c:
Debit: Assets ₹10,00,000 + Creditors paid ₹3,90,000 + Fee to A ₹20,000 = ₹14,10,000.
Credit: Creditors ₹4,00,000 + Sale proceeds ₹8,50,000 = ₹12,50,000.
Loss = ₹14,10,000 − ₹12,50,000 = ₹1,60,000.
B's share = 2/6 × ₹1,60,000 = ₹53,333 (approx).

Wait, let me recalculate. The agreed fee is ₹20,000 debited to Realisation (actual expenses are A's problem).
Debit: Assets 10,00,000 + Creditors paid 3,90,000 + Fee 20,000 = 14,10,000.
Credit: Creditors 4,00,000 + Sale 8,50,000 = 12,50,000.
Loss = 1,60,000. B = 2/6 = 1/3 × 1,60,000 = ₹53,333.

Let me simplify to nice numbers. Actually the loss works out correctly.

B's share of loss = 2/6 × ₹1,60,000 = ₹53,333.

Hmm, let me adjust numbers for cleaner output. Actually let me keep it and round properly: 1,60,000 × 2/6 = 3,20,000/6 = 53,333.33. That's not clean.

Let me redo: Make expenses ₹18,000 agreed fee ₹24,000.
Debit: 10,00,000 + 3,90,000 + 24,000 = 14,14,000.
Credit: 4,00,000 + 8,50,000 = 12,50,000.
Loss = 1,64,000. Still not clean.

Let me use: Assets 9,00,000, Creditors 3,00,000. Assets realised 7,50,000. Creditors paid 2,85,000. Fee 30,000.
Debit: 9,00,000 + 2,85,000 + 30,000 = 12,15,000.
Credit: 3,00,000 + 7,50,000 = 10,50,000.
Loss = 1,65,000. B = 2/6 × 1,65,000 = 55,000. Clean!
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,65,000 is the total loss. B bears only 2/6 of it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assigning entire loss to one partner.

question_text: On dissolution, when a creditor accepts an asset in full settlement of their claim, the entry is:
explanation:
When a creditor takes an asset instead of cash: the creditor's account is settled (debited) and Realisation A/c is credited with the value of the liability discharged. No cash changes hands. Entry: Creditor A/c (or Realisation A/c for creditor payment side) adjustment. The simplest entry: No separate entry is needed beyond the transfers already made, as both the asset and liability are already in Realisation. But if asked specifically: Realisation A/c Dr. (nil net effect since both asset and liability cancel out within Realisation).

Actually the correct treatment: Since both assets and liabilities are already transferred to Realisation, when a creditor takes an asset, no additional entry is needed in books — the settlement happens outside the books. The Realisation Account automatically captures the net effect.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Creditor's A/c Dr. → To Asset A/c
Realisation A/c Dr. → To Cash A/c
No additional entry is needed as both are already in Realisation Account
Cash A/c Dr. → To Realisation A/c

elimination_hints.hint: Individual Creditor and Asset accounts have already been closed by transfer to Realisation. They no longer exist.
elimination_hints.misconception: Trying to use accounts already closed.

elimination_hints.hint: No cash is involved in this transaction — the creditor takes an asset, not cash.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming cash payment when assets are given.

elimination_hints.hint: No cash is received. The creditor gets an asset directly, so Cash A/c is not affected.

question_text: On dissolution, provision for doubtful debts (₹10,000) and debtors (₹1,00,000) appear in the Balance Sheet. Debtors realised ₹85,000. In the Realisation Account, debtors are transferred at:
explanation: Both debtors (₹1,00,000) and provision for doubtful debts (₹10,000) are transferred to Realisation Account. Debtors (asset) go to debit side at ₹1,00,000. Provision (contra-asset/liability) goes to credit side at ₹10,000. The amount realised (₹85,000) is also credited. Net effect on Realisation = Debit ₹1,00,000 − Credit (₹10,000 + ₹85,000) = ₹5,000 loss.
subject: accountancy

options.text: ₹1,00,000 (gross, debit side); Provision ₹10,000 on credit side separately
₹90,000 (net of provision) on debit side only
₹1,00,000 on credit side and ₹85,000 on debit side

elimination_hints.hint: Net transfer is an alternative method but not the standard. The standard is to transfer both separately — debtors at gross value, provision separately.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using net method instead of standard gross transfer.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹85,000 is the amount realised, not the transfer value. Assets are transferred at BOOK value.
elimination_hints.misconception: Transferring at realised value instead of book value.

elimination_hints.hint: Debtors (an asset) go on the debit side, not credit. Credit side shows liabilities and receipts.

question_text:
Match the following items with their treatment in Realisation Account on dissolution:

| Column I (Item) | Column II (Treatment) |
|---|---|
| (i) Assets at book value | (p) Credited to Realisation A/c |
| (ii) External liabilities at book value | (q) Debited to Realisation A/c |
| (iii) Sale proceeds of assets | (r) Not transferred to Realisation A/c |
| (iv) Cash/Bank balance | (s) Credited to Realisation A/c |

Choose the correct matching:
explanation:
(i) Assets at book value → Debited to Realisation → (q)
(ii) External liabilities at book value → Credited to Realisation → (p)
(iii) Sale proceeds → Credited to Realisation (Cash Dr, Realisation Cr) → (s)
(iv) Cash/Bank → NOT transferred to Realisation → (r)
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Assets go to debit side (q), not credit (p). When we close asset accounts, we credit them and debit Realisation.
(ii) and (iii) are both credits, but (ii) is liabilities transfer (p) and (iii) is sale proceeds (s). Both are credit but different items.
Assets (i) are definitely transferred to Realisation (debited), not excluded (r). Cash is the one excluded.

question_text: On dissolution, if the Realisation Account shows: Total debits ₹15,40,000 and total credits ₹14,00,000, and partners A, B, C share in 5:3:2, the entry to close Realisation Account is:
explanation: Debit total > Credit total → Loss of ₹1,40,000. Loss is transferred to partners' capitals in PSR 5:3:2. A = 5/10 × 1,40,000 = ₹70,000. B = 3/10 × 1,40,000 = ₹42,000. C = 2/10 × 1,40,000 = ₹28,000. Entry: A's Capital Dr. 70,000, B's Capital Dr. 42,000, C's Capital Dr. 28,000 → To Realisation A/c 1,40,000.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Realisation A/c Dr. → To Partners' Capital A/c (₹70,000; ₹42,000; ₹28,000)
Partners' Capital A/c Dr. (equally) → To Realisation A/c
Cash A/c Dr. → To Realisation A/c ₹1,40,000
A's Capital Dr. ₹70,000, B's Capital Dr. ₹42,000, C's Capital Dr. ₹28,000 → To Realisation A/c

elimination_hints.hint: This credits partners — which means profit distribution. But there's a LOSS here (debits > credits). Partners should be debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a loss as a profit.

elimination_hints.hint: Loss is shared in PSR (5:3:2), not equally. Each partner bears their proportionate share.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing loss equally instead of PSR.

elimination_hints.hint: Cash is not involved in closing Realisation. The balance is transferred to partners' capital accounts.
